blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Something isn't right.
He said it simply, the way you say a thing you've been not-saying for long enough that the not-saying has become its own kind of weight.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Not with us. With Cassandra. There's something she's carrying that I can't see. I can feel the outline of it — the shape of the space around it — but not the thing itself. And the people who love her know. Her sister knows. Her father knows. They've both spoken to me in this particular way — carefully, not to alarm, not to explain. Just to prepare me for something.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: And you don't know what it is.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: No.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Have you asked her?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Not directly. She knows I sense it. I told her — on her porch, after dinner — that I notice. That I'm not pushing. That I'll wait for her to be ready.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: And how did she respond?
He thought about it honestly.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: She said: 'I know. That's what makes it harder.'
Mr. Alvarez was very still. His expression was the one he had when he was deciding not what to do, but how to say something he had already understood.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: And you've been carrying that.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Since Saturday.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Four days.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Four days.
A quiet beat. The grinder ran briefly in the back.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Ethan. Can I ask you something?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yes.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: When you are with her — when she is in front of you and things are easy between you, and the difficult thing is not in the room — how do you feel?
He didn't have to think about that one.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Like I'm exactly where I'm supposed to be.
